text: The surgeon releases free air through the umbilical port and establishes pneumoperitoneum at twelve millimeters of mercury pressure.
Three additional laparoscopic ports are inserted: a 10mm port at the umbilicus level, a 5mm port in the left iliac fossa, and a 5mm port in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon explores and assesses the site of perforation on the transverse colon, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a tattoo to the colon, marking the site for future reference, with the ink visibly spreading on the tissue surface.

The surgeon assesses an AAST grade II injury on the bowel, which is less than four centimeters in length. The injury appears well vascularized and mobile, with minimal contamination visible in the prepared bowel.
The surgeon freshens the margin of the perforation on the transverse colon, preparing the tissue for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon uses a 4x3 inch swab to clean the margin of the perforation on the transverse colon, soaking up visible blood from the area.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing of the perforation using 3-0 PDS sutures, applying interrupted and full-thickness stitches to close the defect.
The surgeon places the first two sutures, starting at the far end of the perforation and then at the near end, using intracorporeal technique.
The surgeon removes both sutures behind the port on the right side, using them as stay sutures to maintain tension and position during the procedure.
The surgeon places three additional interrupted full-thickness sutures at the perforation site, using intracorporeal technique with 3-0 PDS sutures.
The surgeon places an additional suture at the furthermost end of the perforation, correcting the initial placement of the first suture.
The surgeon ties off the intracorporeal knots on the sutures, starting with the far end, followed by the near end, and finally the middle sutures, completing the closure of the perforation.
The surgeon ties off the first two sutures with the needle still attached, but then removes the needle for tying subsequent sutures, facilitating easier manipulation.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal knot tying using the C and reverse C technique, securing the sutures within the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the Thorola Bhaj procedure, starting at the site of the perforation. The process then progresses towards the left upper quadrant and centrally around the small bowel loops in the inter-loop area.
The surgeon navigates the pelvis, right side of the abdomen, right sub-hepatic, and right supranic areas, before returning to the site of the perforation for further examination or intervention.
A size twenty-eight Wallis drain is placed on the left side of the abdomen and secured in position.
